duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Zero-value transfers to approved addresses with zero balance bypass the pre-transfer balanceOf existence check in _beforeTokenTransfer, pushing duplicate entries into the holders array. During distribution, each duplicate entry pays full entitlement, enabling reward pool drain.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 13 threats describe the same code defect: _beforeTokenTransfer checks balanceOf(to) pre-transfer and pushes to holders[] when zero, allowing unlimited duplicate entries via 0-value transfers that never change the balance. Impact is reward multiplication theft and potential permanent distribution lock. ECON-H001 selected for most comprehensive attack economics and impact analysis.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Every burn operation triggers _beforeTokenTransfer with to=address(0). Since balanceOf(address(0)) is always 0, address(0) is pushed to holders[] on every burn. These entries are never removed, causing unbounded array growth and eventual distribution DoS via gas exhaustion.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 13 threats describe the same root cause: _beforeTokenTransfer unconditionally pushes address(0) to holders on every burn because balanceOf(address(0))==0 always. Variants include different entry points (burn, burnFrom, burnAndDistribute(0), burnFromAndDistribute(0), allowance-based burning) but all exploit the same code defect. TECH-H002 selected for clearest technical explanation of the root cause and cleanup failure path.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: releaseManagedNFT uses require(true) instead of a boolean tracking whether the NFT was found and removed from ManagedNFTs, silently succeeding even when the NFT is not in the array.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All three describe the same literal code defect: require(true, 'unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs') always passes regardless of whether the NFT was found. TECH-H003 provides the most detailed analysis of downstream consequences.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: The swap-and-pop loop in _afterTokenTransfer lacks a break statement after removal, causing the element swapped from the array end into position i to be skipped when i increments, preventing proper cleanup of duplicate entries.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All six describe the identical missing-break bug in _afterTokenTransfer's swap-and-pop loop. Same code location, same mechanism, same consequence (incomplete holder removal compounding the address(0) and duplicate entry bugs). TECH-H004 selected for most precise technical description.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Integer division in _beginDistribution calculates entitlementPerUnit as balance/supply. For low-decimal tokens (USDC/USDT with 6 decimals) and 18-decimal ERC20 supply, the division truncates to 0, permanently locking all revenue of that token.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All eight describe the same division-before-multiplication precision loss in entitlement = balance / supply. Same code location (_beginDistribution), same root cause (integer division truncation), same impact (revenue permanently locked for low-decimal tokens). TECH-H005 selected for comprehensive technical analysis with specific numeric examples and config evidence.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: setDistributableERC20s lacks a LockedForDistribution check, allowing the admin to change the distributableERC20s array mid-distribution. This causes array length mismatch with erc20EntitlementPerUnit, leading to out-of-bounds revert and permanent contract lock.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All three describe the same missing guard: setDistributableERC20s can be called while LockedForDistribution is true, causing array size mismatch that permanently bricks the contract. TECH-H006 selected for most detailed explanation of the permanent lock mechanism.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: releaseManagedNFT uses swap-and-pop on ManagedNFTs without adjusting nextWithdrawal. Called during a partial withdrawal cycle, this corrupts the withdrawal cursor, skipping NFTs or permanently stalling withdrawals if array length drops below nextWithdrawal.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All six describe the same state corruption: releaseManagedNFT modifies ManagedNFTs array without adjusting the nextWithdrawal cursor used by withdrawFromManagedNFTs. Same root cause, same consequence (skipped NFTs or permanent withdrawal stall). TECH-H007 selected for most comprehensive analysis including the permanent stall scenario.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Entitlement calculation uses totalSupply() (including disapproved holders' balances) as denominator, but distribute() skips disapproved holders. Their proportional share is permanently trapped, diluting approved holders' yield.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All five describe the same accounting mismatch: totalSupply includes disapproved holders for entitlement calculation, but distribution skips them, trapping their share. Same code locations (_beginDistribution and distribute), same mechanism and impact. TECH-H008 selected for most thorough explanation of the persistent dilution effect.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: distribute() uses raw IERC20.transfer() without SafeERC20. Tokens that revert on transfer (USDT non-bool return causing ABI decode failure, blacklisted recipients, paused tokens) cause distribute() to revert while LockedForDistribution is true, permanently bricking the contract.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All seven describe the same core defect: distribute() performs external ERC20 transfer() calls inside a locked state with no SafeERC20, try/catch, or skip mechanism. Whether the revert is caused by USDT's missing bool return, a blacklisted recipient, or a paused token, the consequence is identical: permanent contract lock. TECH-H014 selected for broadest coverage of revert scenarios and clearest impact chain.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: In distribute(), the conditional if(toDistribute.transfer(recipient, entitlement)) silently skips payouts when transfer returns false (instead of reverting), permanently forfeiting the holder's entitlement for that cycle with no recourse or event indication.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All four describe the same silent failure mode: ERC20 transfer returning false is handled as a no-op rather than a revert, silently losing the holder's entitlement. Same code location (distribute loop), same mechanism and impact. ECON-H011 selected for most detailed analysis of the economic impact and lack of recourse. Note: TECH2-H005 refers to the second instance with this ID (title: 'Silent reward loss...').

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: When MinDistributionPeriod=0, _isPastMinDistributionPeriod() returns true immediately after any distribution (0>=0), causing _beforeMintOrBurn to always revert. Distribute completes instantly but resets LastDistribution, creating an unsatisfiable loop that permanently deadlocks minting and burning.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All six describe the identical edge case: MinDistributionPeriod=0 makes the >= check always true, creating an infinite deadlock on mint/burn. Same code locations, same mechanism, same impact. ECON-H006 selected for clearest explanation of the deadlock loop and config immutability.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Public withdrawFromManagedNFTs makes external calls to each ManagedNFT without try/catch. A single reverting NFT (stale entry, lost ownership, non-conforming contract) blocks the persistent nextWithdrawal cursor from advancing, permanently stalling revenue collection.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All three describe the same withdrawal griefing/DoS via reverting ManagedNFT entries blocking the batched withdrawal cursor. Same code path (withdrawFromManagedNFTs iterating with external calls and no error handling). TECH-H015 selected for most complete analysis. Note: TECH2-H006 here refers to the second instance with this ID (title: 'Public withdrawal batching...').

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Withdrawal from managed NFTs uses getThresholds() to determine which ERC20s to withdraw. Tokens held by the NFT but not in thresholdErc20s are never withdrawn. After NFT transfer to the ERC20 contract, thresholds become effectively immutable since the ERC20 contract has no function to call setThresholds.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All three describe the same design flaw: withdrawal token list is coupled to thresholds, and tokens not in the list are stranded. ECON2-H017 selected as it additionally identifies the critical immutability angle (ERC20 contract cannot update thresholds on owned NFTs). Note: TECH2-H007 here refers to the second instance with this ID (title: 'Threshold list manipulation...').
